topic: Patrick Jr's first real fire — proud and terrified

turns.content: my son had his first real fire last night. I mean a real one. not a dumpster or a car. a working house fire.
That's a big milestone. How did he do?
he did good. real good from what I heard. made the push with his crew, stayed on the line, did his job. his captain called me this morning and said he was solid.
That's got to feel good to hear, especially from his captain. Is your son a firefighter too?
yeah. followed me onto the job. he's with the PFD. I'm proud of him. but I'm also terrified. and I wasn't ready for how those two things could hit at the same time.
That's a unique position to be in — you know exactly what he walked into because you've done it yourself. Most parents worry from a place of imagination. You worry from experience. That's harder.

that's it exactly. I know what fire does. I know how fast things go wrong. I've seen it. so when I got the text from him that said "had my first worker tonight" my stomach dropped before my brain even processed the words.

just "had my first worker tonight. went good. I'm fine." three sentences. kid's not exactly a poet. I called him right away. he was amped up. talking fast. telling me about the push and the conditions and the overhaul. sounded just like I did 30 years ago after my first one.
That full circle moment — hearing your son sound exactly like you did at his age. That's something. Did you give him any advice or just let him talk?
I let him talk. he needed to get it out. then I told him the same thing I tell every young guy. stay low, stay on the wall, and don't be a hero. the job is the job. you do it right and you come home.
Good advice. Simple and clear. Did Marie get to talk to him too?

oh yeah. she got on the phone after me. she was crying. happy crying but still. I could hear her telling him to be careful. he was laughing and saying ma I'm fine. same conversation she had with me a thousand times.
Sounds like history repeating in the best way. He made it through his first real test, his captain vouched for him, and he called his parents. That's a good kid.
he is a good kid. I just gotta figure out how to sleep at night knowing he's out there doing what I do. it's different when it's your son.

It is different. And I don't think that part gets easier — you just get better at carrying it. But he's trained, he's got good people around him, and he's got a father who knows the job inside and out. That's more than most rookies have going for them.
affirmative. thanks for listening. needed to get that off my chest.
Anytime. You've got a lot to be proud of. Tell him his old man said good work.
